Seven Lifers Before Breakfast

Monday, May 25, 2026 · 7 species· 20 detections ★ 7 life-list first

The olive-sided flycatcher is a small to medium sized passerine bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatcher family.

Some mornings you walk outside half-awake and the woods just hand you a gift. This was one of those mornings — a seven-minute window around 6:40 where my list went from familiar to almost unrecognizable. Every single species today was new to me.

The headliner was an Olive-sided Flycatcher, calling clear and confident enough that I didn't have to squint at the confidence score to believe it. That bird has been on my "someday" list forever — the kind of pewee-cousin you read about perched at the tip of a snag, hawking insects over a clearing. And today, finally, here.

Sharing the soundscape was a MacGillivray's Warbler, which feels like the perfect companion lifer: a skulky Pacific Northwest specialty I've been hoping to pick up for ages. Hearing those two together, in the same handful of minutes, felt almost suspicious — like the dawn chorus had read my wishlist.

Rounding out the morning was a Spotted Towhee scratching its way into the mix, plus a tidy little cohort of warblers and vireos I'd never logged before. One scrub-jay made a cameo, loud and unmistakable as scrub-jays always are. Even the Hermit Warbler showed up, though faintly enough that I'm holding it loosely until I can confirm — a 37% nudge from the algorithm is more of an invitation than a verdict.

What I love about mornings like this is how compressed it all was. The birds were already awake and busy before I'd fully committed to being a person. If I'd hit snooze one more time, half of this would've been gone by the time I made it to the window.

I keep thinking about the Olive-sided though. Of all of them, that's the one I want to see, not just hear. Now that I know they're around, I've got a target for the next walk: find the highest dead branch in the neighborhood and just stare at it until something pewee-shaped lands.

Seven lifers in one dawn. I don't expect to repeat that anytime soon, so I'm going to sit with it for a while. Gallery's below — go listen to the MacGillivray's especially, it's such a satisfying little song.

Good birding, a very caffeinated me

Today's cast

MacGillivray's Warbler
Geothlypis tolmiei
×5 · 86% best confidence · 06:36–06:43 ★ first ✓ local

MacGillivray's warbler is a species of New World warbler. These birds are sluggish and heavy warblers, preferring to spend most of their time on, or near the ground, except when singing.

California Scrub-Jay
Aphelocoma californica
×1 · 88% best confidence · 06:41 ★ first ✓ local

The California scrub jay is a species of scrub jay native to western North America. It ranges from southern British Columbia throughout California and western Nevada near Reno to west of the Sierra Nevada.

Hermit Warbler
Setophaga occidentalis
×1 · 38% best confidence · 06:37 ★ first ✓ local

The hermit warbler is a small perching bird. It is a species of New World warbler or wood-warbler. They are a migratory bird, the breeding range spanning the majority of the west coast of the United States.
